coredata.dc:title: Bimetallic ZnNi co-catalyst modified g-C3N4 nanosheets for highly efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Li-Juan
Jia, Yan-Ming
Yang, Fu
Bai, Zhi-Yan
Xie, Yu-Long
coredata.dc:description: In this work, bimetallic ZnNi/g-C3N4 (ZnNi/CN) photocatalysts prepared by chemical reduction method were investigated for their photocatalytic hydrogen production activity, and it was found that the addition of the bimetallic ZnNi promotes the reactive active site, improves the charge transfer efficiency, and inhibits the ability of photogenerated electron-hole complexes effectively. Therefore, the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst with the best hydrogen production activity can produce up to 1822.75 μmol g−1 h−1 of hydrogen, which is 56.8 times higher than that of the conventional g-C3N4 (CN) photocatalyst. The results suggest that the effective combination of bimetallic ZnNi and CN materials can enhance the photocatalytic activity for hydrogen production. This work demonstrates the great potential of bimetallic co-catalysts and provides new insights into the efficient hydrogen production by modified CN nanosheets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: G-C3N4
Bimetallic ZnNi
Co-catalyst
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of CN and ZnNi/CN photocatalyst. (b) FTIR spectra of 1%–5% ZnNi/CN and CN.
SEM images of (a) CN, (b)Zn/CN, (c) Ni/CN, (d) 3% ZnNi/CN, (e)–(i) EDS-mapping element distribution of 3% ZnNi/CN.
(a) TEM images, (b) and (c)HRTEM images, (d) inverse FFT patterns of 3% ZnNi/CN, (e–i) HRTEM images EDS-mapping of 3% ZnNi/CN.
(a) and (b) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption curves and pore size distribution of pure CN with 3% ZnNi/CN.
(a) The XPS spectrums in C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) Zn 2p, (d) Ni 2p of the 3% ZnNi/CN.
(a) UV–vis DRS reflectance spectra of CN, Zn/CN, Ni/CN and ZnNi/CN photocatalysts, (b) Tauc band gap of CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst.
(a) PL emission spectra, (b) TRPL spectra stimulated at 360 nm, (c) Transient response curves for photocurrent, (d) EIS plots, (e) The Mott-Schottky plot, (f) Band structure of CN and 3% ZnNi/CN.

(a) Transient photocatalytic H2 production rate, (b) The average H2 evolution rate of the CN, Zn/CN, Ni/CN and ZnNi/CN photocatalysts, (c) Five-cycle experiment of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts, (d) The 3% ZnNi/CN XRD pattern before and after five consecutive photocatalytic reactions.
Schematic representation of charge transfer in ZnNi/g-C3N4 under visible light.
Flowchart for the fabrication of ZnNi/CN nanosheet.
SBET, pore volume and average pore size of CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Bimetallic ZnNi co-catalyst modified g-C3N4 nanosheets for highly efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Qinghai Minzu University, Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry and Eco-environmental Protection in Tibetan Plateau of State Ethnic Affairs Commission, Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology of Qinghai Province, Xining, Qinghai, 810007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science
Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry and Eco-environmental Protection in Tibetan Plateau of State Ethnic Affairs Commission
Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology of Qinghai Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Qinghai Minzu University, Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry and Eco-environmental Protection in Tibetan Plateau of State Ethnic Affairs Commission, Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology of Qinghai Province, Xining, Qinghai, 810007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, bimetallic ZnNi/g-C3N4 (ZnNi/CN) photocatalysts prepared by chemical reduction method were investigated for their photocatalytic hydrogen production activity, and it was found that the addition of the bimetallic ZnNi promotes the reactive active site, improves the charge transfer efficiency, and inhibits the ability of photogenerated electron-hole complexes effectively. Therefore, the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst with the best hydrogen production activity can produce up to 1822.75 μmol g−1 h−1 of hydrogen, which is 56.8 times higher than that of the conventional g-C3N4 (CN) photocatalyst. The results suggest that the effective combination of bimetallic ZnNi and CN materials can enhance the photocatalytic activity for hydrogen production. This work demonstrates the great potential of bimetallic co-catalysts and provides new insights into the efficient hydrogen production by modified CN nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:para: The modern world is experiencing rapid social growth, opportunities and challenges face by all facets of development, the need for improved living conditions is growing, and the use of new energy sources is becoming more and more necessary [1,2].Especially in industrial development, some factories discharge industrial effluent and untreated wastewater containing harmful substances into nature, which has a great impact on people's lives and material needs [3,4]. Industrial wastewater discharges are exacerbating energy problems and environmental pollution, which need to be further addressed [5,6]. The use of energy and the protection of the environment are closely related to human life, so there is a need to reduce water pollution and actively develop renewable energy. We take into account the degree of difficulty associated with these issues, and a new technology is desperately needed to address the difficulties and issues encountered [7,8]. Utilizing photocatalytic technology [9] to address the issues of energy scarcity and environmental contamination is a successful tactic. Photocatalytic technology has emerged as a new technology for problem-solving in recent years [10,11].

The modern world is in dire need of new and affordable sources of energy, such as solar, wind, and hydrogen, all of which are very efficient and green [12,13]. Since the process of producing hydrogen energy by photocatalytic water decomposition is superior, it is critical to create photocatalysts that are easy to prepare, more stable, and free of secondary pollutants [14–16]. Among a variety of photocatalysts, graphitic phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is utilized as an inexpensive and readily accessible material because of its unique electronic configuration and exceptional photoelectric properties [17,18]. g-C3N4 is a photocatalyst that differs from many other catalysts in that it has good stability, an easy preparation method, and excellent hydrogen-producing performance [13]. And many of the substances, such as urea [19], thiourea [20], melamine, etc., can be used to prepare g-C3N4 nanosheets. There are some drawbacks to the photocatalytic g-C3N4 hydrogen production performance, though. These include a low rate of light utilization [21], a sluggish charge transfer rate [22], an easy complexation of photogenerated electron holes, and a lack of active sites [23,24]. Numerous researchers have suggested a variety of modification techniques, including elemental doping [25,26], semiconductor compounding [27], co-catalyst modification [28,29], and heterostructure construction [15,30], to address these issues and improve some of the shortcomings in g-C3N4's hydrogen production performance.

Co-catalyst modification has drawn the interest of numerous researchers, who have discovered that co-catalysts can address issues like slow charge transfer and insufficient active sites [11], in addition to increasing the efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production. For example, some transition metal (Zn, Ni, etc.) nanoparticles act as co-catalysts, which can significantly improve the active site deficiency and enhance the charge transfer efficiency, etc., and can improve the efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production. Rugma et al. [31] report NiO/g-C3N4 photocatalysts were synthesized through calcination and wet impregnation for enhancing photocatalytic activity, in which NiO was used as a co-catalyst for modifying CN nanosheets to effectively improve light absorption and decompose aqueous hydrogen. Zhao et al. [32] studied the NiS/g-C3N4 photocatalyst, in which NiS as a hydrogen-producing co-catalyst can effectively inhibit the photogenerated electron-hole pair complexation and increase the hydrogen release. Shi et al. [33] prepared (ZnO@g-C3N4) composite membranes for photocatalytic degradation by in situ growth method, which improved the photocatalytic responsiveness and degradation performance, in which the degradation performance reached 94.4%. To improve the effectiveness of photocatalytic hydrogen production, bimetallic co-catalysts can have more active sites and a higher charge transfer efficiency than monometallic co-catalysts. Lu et al. [13] reported the preparation of bimetallic CoNi/g-C3N4 photocatalysts by chemical and solvothermal reduction techniques, it was discovered that the bimetallic CoNi increased charge transfer efficiency and hasten the rapid separation of photogenerated electrons. The hydrogen production efficiency of the CoNi/g-C3N4 photocatalysts was 354.4 μmol h−1g−1, 188 times greater than the initial g-C3N4 value in comparison to the pristine g-C3N4 value. As a result, bimetals can effectively prevent the rapid complexation of charges and holes, increase the forbidden bandwidth, and enhance light absorption [34]. Furthermore, bimetals as co-catalysts offer greater research opportunities for enhancing the efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production.

In order to prepare a photocatalyst with high stability and hydrogen production efficiency, it is proposed to modify g-C3N4 nanosheets with bimetallic ZnNi using a chemical reduction method to maximize the photocatalytic hydrogen production performance of ZnNi/CN under visible light conditions. The best-performing 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst was investigated, with a hydrogen production efficiency of (1822.75 μmol h−1 g−1), which was 56.8 times higher than that of the pristine CN (32.04 μmol h−1 g−1) nanosheets. In addition to this, there is excellent stability, which does not deteriorate after 5 cycles. Based on a series of characterisations showing that the modification of bimetallic ZnNi improves charge transfer and generates active reactive sites, the potential photocatalytic hydrogen mechanism production was investigated.

Melamine (C₃H₆N₆) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Zn(CH₃COO)₂(H₂O)₂ was purchased from Shanghai Guangnuo Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. N2H4·H2O was purchased from Nanjing Huangtai Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. NaOH was purchased from Hongyan Reagent Factory, Hedong District, Tianjin, China. All chemicals can be used directly without further purification.

Melamine is thermally polymerized in a muffle furnace to yield g-C3N4 (CN). 10 g of melamine were burned in a muffle furnace for 4 h at a rate of 2.3 °C·min−1 at 550 °C after being placed in a covered crucible. After that, the muffle furnace cools naturally to room temperature, the light-yellow g-C3N4 samples were then collected and ground into a fine powder. Following that, It was cleaned three times with ethanol and three times with water. The last sample is dried for 4 h at 80 °C in a vacuum oven (Scheme 1 ).

The ZnNi/CN photocatalyst was prepared by chemical reduction method. Firstly, 4 mmol of CN was weighed and added into deionized water, ultrasonically stirred for 30 min. The 2 mL of nickel acetate and zinc acetate solution with a concentration of 0.01 M (the molar ratio of Zn:Ni was 1:1) was added, and stirred for 4 h. Then, 6 mL of N2H4·H2O (0.05 M) solution and 4 mL of NaOH (0.1 M) were added to the above suspension. After 2 h of thorough stirring, it was then moved to a high-pressure reactor and maintained for 3 h at 150 °C in an oven.

After the reactor was cooled to room temperature, it was rinsed several times with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water and filtered, and then dried in a vacuum drying oven, the samples were collected and recorded as x-ZnNi/g-C3N4 (x-ZnNi/CN), where x is the weight percentage of ZnNi (1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 wt%). The same procedure was used to prepare 3% Zn/CN (Zn/CN) and 3% Ni/CN (Ni/CN), As shown in Scheme 1.

Utilizing Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) to investigate chemical alterations and surface structure. Using powder X-ray diffraction, the material's crystalline phase structure was investigated (XRD). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms are being used to determine the specific surface area and pore size distribution of materials. UV–Vis Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS) was used to assess the reflectance of the catalysts. The morphology on the catalyst microstructure was described using field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was used to examine and assess the samples' elemental distribution, while transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was engaged to evaluate the morphology and lattice fringing of the materials. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to ascertain the catalysts' surface bonding status. The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectrum is excited at room temperature using a wavelength of 360 nm.

An electrochemical workstation equipped with a three-electrode system was used for the photocurrent (PC) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. PC and EIS in the studies employed an electrolyte solution of 0.5 M Na2SO4. The sample was set up on ITO glass, a platinum (Pt) grid served as the counter electrode, and a conventional saturated calomel electrode served as the reference electrode. The working electrode was the ZnNi/CN photocatalyst powder electrode on the ITO electrode. The saturated calomel electrode was used to record all potentials.

The H2 production performances of the photocatalysts were studied using a photocatalytic activity evaluation system with a quartz reactor (CEL-PAEM-D8, Beijing China Education Au-Light Co., Ltd.). The 300 W Xe lamp was used as the simulated sunlight source in the photocatalytic reaction system. Specifically, disperse 50 mg of catalyst in 50 mL of deionized water. containing a triethanolamine sacrificial agent, and after that, the dispersion was moved to the photoreaction tank and agitated constantly, and the entire apparatus was maintained under vacuum. By using gas chromatography, H2 was found to 0.5 h intervals, and the amount of H2 generated was continuously monitored for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure, morphology and elemental composition
ce:section.ce:para: XRD characterization was used to describe the peak positions of the CN and ZnNi/CN photocatalyst. From Fig. 1 (a), it can be seen that there are obvious peaks at 13.1° and 27.3°, and the peak positions are the classical diffraction peaks of CN, and they correspond to the (001) and (002) crystal planes of CN (PDF# 87–1526), and these samples have preserved the original structure of CN, which indicates that the material has been successfully prepared [35]. However, no peaks were observed for Zn and Ni, probably due to reduced content and lower crystallinity, which were not detected by the equipment, but from the (002) diffraction peak enlargement, it can be seen that the Zn/CN and ZnNi/CN samples were slightly shifted to the left with respect to Ni/CN and CN, and with the Zn content rises, the amplitude of the diffraction peaks shifted to the left gradually increased [36]. However, the content of Ni has no significant effect on the shift of the (002) diffraction peaks, which may be caused by the lower content of Ni and the more dispersed Ni. It is further shown that the bimetallic ZnNi is successfully dispersed on CN.

For further observation of the samples, Fig. 1(b) shows the FTIR spectra of CN and Zn/CN, Ni/CN, ZnNi/CN samples within the scope of 400–4000 cm−1 [37]. The stretching vibrations of the C–N bonds of the CN heterocycles are represented by the wide peaks at 3000-3400 cm−1 [38], the classical N–H bonds of CN or the O–H bonds of water by the wide peaks at 1200-1600 cm−1 [39], and the triazine unit's usual respiration mode by the acute peaks at 810 cm−1. All of the ZnNi/CN samples exhibit the same spectral signals, and Zn/CN and Ni/CN likewise exhibit the same spectral signals. This suggests that the manufacture of the bimetallic ZnNi and CN photocatalysts was successful.

The morphology and elemental content were studied using energy spectroscopy and SEM. As can be seen in Fig. 2 (a), CN exhibits an irregularly coiled nanosheet structure pattern, and Fig. 2(b) Zn/CN exhibits a blocky and agglomerated structure, suggesting that the original morphology of CN is altered with the addition of Zn. The Ni/CN has an agglomerated and blocky structure, as seen in Fig. 2(c). The 3% ZnNi/CN is showed in Fig. 2(d), and the images demonstrate the blocky and nano-flake modifications, it is shown that the incorporation of the bimetallic ZnNi changes the morphology of the CN photocatalysts. In addition, Fig. 2(e)-2(i) show the EDS mapping images of the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts to determine the elemental distributions therein. It is evident that the elements in the 3% ZnNi/CN sample are uniformly distributed and impurity-free, with each element showing a distinct distribution signal: C, N, Zn, and N. The outcomes show that the preparation of the bimetallic ZnNi and CN nanosheets was successful.

Further observation of morphological changes of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts using TEM. Fig. 3 (a) displays the HRTEM image of 3% ZnNi/CN, demonstrating there are nanoparticles present on the CN surface and the successful distribution of the bimetallic ZnNi on CN nanosheets. Fig. 3(a) and 3(b) illustrate the different magnifications [40], it is further shown that the successful preparation of bimetallic ZnNi/CN composites. Showed Fig. 3(d), as can be seen, the ZnNi alloys' (101) crystallographic plane corresponds to the lattice stripe spacing of 0.259 nm, indicating the successful preparation of the ZnNi/CN sample. The 3(e)-3(i) diagram offers a more detailed structural analysis of the EDS-mapping image. It highlights the C, N, Zn, and Ni elements, indicating their presence. Additionally, this indicates that the distribution of Zn and Ni elements in the material, suggesting that the bimetallic ZnNi and CN nanosheets were successfully synthesized.

In addition, Fig. 4 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples, and Fig. 4(a) and 4(b) show that both CN and 3% ZnNi/CN have type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, suggesting that there is a porous structure in this photocatalyst. The pore size distribution curves in Fig. 4(a) and 4(b) show that the pore sizes of the CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts are distributed around 20 nm. This further suggests that the photocatalysts have a porous structure that is favourable to charge transport. Table 1 shows the SBET, porosity and average pore size of CN and 3% ZnNi/CN composites. According to Table 1, the specific surface area and pore volume of 3% ZnNi/CN are 48.02 m2/g and 0.21 cm2/g, respectively. whereas CN has 73.98 m2/g and 0.30 cm2/g, respectively. As a result, 3% ZnNi/CN has a smaller particular surface area and volume of pores than CN [41,42]. The reason is that due to the addition of bimetallic ZnNi supplementing part of the pore size of CN and bringing the two into closer contact, the porous structure can provide more reactive active sites, which can shorten the distance of charge transfer and further improve the photocatalytic activity.

The chemical states and elemental compositions of the surfaces of the CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts were characterised using XPS, and the C 1s peaks were adjusted to the standard peak positions. As can be seen from Fig. 5 (a), the high-resolution C 1s spectra show three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.2 eV for CN, and three peaks at positions 284.7 eV, 286.3 and 288.2 eV for 3% ZnNi/CN. The characteristic peak at 284.80 eV consists of sp2 hybridised C–C [43], and the peaks at 288.2 and 286.3 are associated with the triazine ring NC–N and sp3 coordinated carbon (C–N) [44]. The C 1s spectra are not significantly shifted, and the catalysts were successfully prepared. The N 1s spectrum of CN as shown in Fig. 5(b) has distinct outgoing peaks at 398.7 eV, 400.3 eV, 401.3 eV, and 404.3 eV. These peaks correspond to CN–C, N-(C)3, –NH2 hybridized N atoms and π excitations, respectively [45,46]. Although the distinctive peaks of CN–C, –NH2 hybridized N atoms and π excitations of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts appeared to be shifted towards higher modification energies, which could be attributed to the reduction in electron density caused by N atoms losing their electrons, suggesting an increase in the charge transfer from CN to the bimetallic ZnNi. The Zn 2p spectrum of the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst exhibits two distinctive peaks at 1021.8 eV and 1045.0 eV positions, respectively. These locations line up with Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 [47], as shown in Fig. 5(c). As can be seen in Fig. 5(d), the Ni 2p spectrum of the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst shows a peak at position 855.8 eV, which corresponds to the Ni2+, the peaks appearing at positions 862.1 and 873.4 are classical signal peaks according to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 of Ni [36,48]. Upon the above results, it was further shown that 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts were successfully prepared and favourable for charge transfer.

The light absorption properties of CN, Zn/CN, Ni/CN, and ZnNi/CN photocatalysts were determined using UV–Vis DRS reflectance spectroscopy, as displayed in From Fig. 6 (a), Evidently, the light absorption limit of CN is around 460 nm, and all the photocatalysts are shifted like a larger wavelength direction than CN, in which the light absorption edge of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts is red-shifted to 475 nm [13], which indicates that the 3% ZnNi/CN has a strong light absorption ability. The range of light absorption rose with the concentration of bimetallic ZnNi, suggesting that the addition of bimetallic ZnNi helped to increase the light absorption capacity, which in turn helped to increase the photocatalytic activity and facilitate the precipitation of hydrogen produced through photocatalysis.

The band gap values of CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts were obtained from the Kubelka-Munk function [49] as shown in Fig. 6(b). The band gap values (Eg) were 2.87 eV and 2.80 eV. It can be seen that the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst has a smaller band gap value with respect to CN, indicating that the addition of the bimetallic ZnNi is beneficial in decreasing the band gap, increasing the ability of light absorption and charge transfer, and generating photogenerated carriers that are favourable for increasing the photocatalytic hydrogen production capacity.

Fig. 7 (a) shows the PL spectra of CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts, which allows the determination of the complexation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs of the samples with a wavelength of excitation of 360 nm [50]. From Fig. 7(a), it can be seen that the CN PL signal intensity reaches the maximum at 460 nm, while the PL signal intensity of 3% ZnNi/CN is very weak, as shown in the inset in Fig. 7(a). The 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst has a lower signal intensity, which can indicate that the material inhibits the rapid compounding of photogenerated electron holes [42,51], and increases the efficiency of the transport of photogenerated electrons. In addition, the time-resolved transient fluorescence spectra of the photocatalysts were also characteristic, and as shown in Fig. 7(b), the 3% ZnNi/CN (1.94 ns) photocatalysts showed faster attenuation and shorter lifetimes compared with the CN (1.57 ns), suggesting that the addition of the bimetallic ZnNi is beneficial in shortening the charge transport with CN and improving charge separation.

To investigate the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers of the photocatalysts, Fig. 7(c) displays the photocurrent response plot. From the figure, it can be seen that both CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts have significant responses. In addition to this, the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst demonstrated a higher photocurrent response, and thus the incorporation of the bimetallic ZnNi resulted in a faster rate of photogenerated carrier transfer and hence increased charge transfer in the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst. Fig. 7(d) shows the electrochemical impedance diagram to study the charge transport and transfer of the catalysts, from which it can be seen that CN has a larger arc radius [7,10], while 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts have a smaller arc radius, indicating that the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts have a smaller charge transfer resistance [52,53], which can increase the rate of charge transfer and thus increase the performance of photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Fig. 7(e) shows the electrochemical Mott-Schottky plots (M − S) to investigate the energy band structure of CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts. From the figure, it can be seen that the CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts present a positive slope in the Mott-Schottky plot, identifying the two materials as n-type semiconductors. The potentials in flat bands of the CN and 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts were determined based on the intercept on the transverse axis, and the parity potentials (Vfb) were determined to be −0.56 eV and −0.41 eV with respect to the saturated calomel electrode (SCE). Use the formula ENHE = ESCE + 0.24 [13,54] to convert the potential of the SCE electrode to a standard hydrogen electrode (NHE), The flat band potentials (Vfb) at NHE are −0.32 eV and −0.17 eV for CN and 3% ZnNi/CN, respectively. Generally, the bottom of the CB is higher than Vfb minus −0.2 V for n-type semiconductors, so that the ECB of CN with respect to NHE is −0.52 eV, and the ECB of 3% ZnNi/CN with respect to NHE is −0.37 eV. The band gaps of CN and 3% ZnNi/CN are calculated to be 2.87 eV and 2.80 eV for CN and 3% ZnNi/CN, respectively, based on the forbidden band widths in Fig. 6(b). In addition, according to the EVB = ECB + Eg Refs. [10,36] formula can be used to obtain the VB for both, so that the EVB for CN with respect to NHE is 2.35 eV, while the EVB for 3% ZnNi/CN with respect to NHE is 2.43 eV. The energy band structure is shown in Fig. 7(f). The CB of the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst is narrower relative to that of CN, suggesting that the addition of the bimetallic ZnNi can increase the charge transport, electron transfer, and thus improve the performance of photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Fig. 8 (a) shows the hydrogen production rates of several samples within 150 min, from which it can be seen that the hydrogen production of several photocatalysts is higher than that of pristine CN, in which the hydrogen production increases and then decreases with the increase of the addition of bimetallic ZnNi, and the hydrogen production of the photocatalysts is higher than that of pristine CN. Among them, the best hydrogen production of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts was achieved at an addition level of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts, indicating that the bimetallic ZnNi-modified CN nanosheets are beneficial for the transport of photogenerated carriers [55], which leads to an increase in the amount of hydrogen production. The decrease in hydrogen production with the increase in bimetallic content to 4% ZnNi/CN and 5% ZnNi/CN may be due to the addition of too much bimetallic ZnNi, which covers the reactive sites therein resulting in the decrease in hydrogen production. In Fig. 8(b), it can be seen that the hydrogen yields were 32.04 μmol g−1 h−1 for CN, 145.87 μmol g−1 h−1 for Zn/CN, 522.87 μmol g−1 h−1 for Ni/CN and 1822.75 μmol g−1 h−1 for 3% ZnNi/CN, which were 56.8 times higher than that of CN, 12.4 times higher than that of Zn/CN and 3.4 times higher than that of Ni/CN, respectively.The above results further demonstrate that bimetallic ZnNi-modified CN nanosheets can serve as important hydrogen-producing photocatalysts.

Five cycles of experiments were used to evaluate the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst's stability and recyclability, as depicted in Fig. 8(c). The catalyst has improved charge separation and transport, more stable photocatalytic hydrogen production, and the ability to be reused, as seen by the figure, the hydrogen production efficiencies after five cycles were 168.43, 168.55, 169.21, 167.54, and 167.14 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively, which shows that there is no discernible change in hydrogen production after five cycles. Therefore, 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst is the best choice for photocatalytic hydrogen production [56]. Following that, samples of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts were collected, and the stability was further evaluated using XRD Fig. 8(d). After five consecutive runs, there is no change in the crystal structure of XRD of 3% ZnNi/CN composite, verifying the 3% ZnNi/CN composite photocatalysts' exceptional stability.

Based on the above characterization results, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic hydrogen production from ZnNi/CN photocatalysts was explored as shown in Fig. 9 . The incorporation of bimetallic ZnNi co-catalysts improves light absorption capacity of the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts and leads to a faster and wider range of photogenerated carrier separation. When irradiated under visible light, photogenerated electron holes are generated on the CN surface, and charges are generated on CB and VB [36]. In addition, the transition metals Zn and Ni have higher Fermi energy levels relative to CN, so the charge transfer from CN to the bimetallic ZnNi will form an interfacial electric field. Under the mutual interaction, the photogenerated carriers will be transferred to the bimetallic ZnNi. Therefore, the e− on the CB of CN are transferred to the CB of bimetallic ZnNi, which has a large negative potential, so that the CB of the bimetallic ZnNi co-catalyst has a large negative potential, and the reaction of water reduction to H2 can be carried out, the h+ on the VB of bimetallic ZnNi is transferred to the VB of CN at the same time. Meanwhile, the addition of the sacrificial agent triethanolamine (TEOA) will immediately trap electron holes at the interface of the 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst for the oxidation of TEOA+ by TEOA [40]. As a result, the synthesis of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalysts encourages the quick separation of electron-hole pairs produced by light and makes charge transfer easier, this increases the photocatalyst's ability to produce hydrogen through the breakdown of water.

In this study, bimetallic ZnNi/CN photocatalysts as well as monometallic Zn/CN and Ni/CN photocatalysts were prepared by chemical reduction method to evaluate their hydrogen production rates. Among them, the hydrogen production of 3% ZnNi/CN photocatalyst was 1822.75 μmol h−1 g−1, which reached the proportional optimum hydrogen production activity, 56.8 times higher than that of pure CN material (32.04 μmol h−1 g−1), and 12.4 times higher than that of Zn/CN material (145.87 μmol h−1 g−1). 12.4 times that of Zn/CN (145.87 μmol h−1 g−1) and 3.4 times that of Ni/CN (522.87 μmol h−1 g−1). The main reason for the significant increase in the hydrogen production activity is that the incorporation of bimetallic ZnNi combined with CN material can effectively promote the rapid separation of photogenerated electron holes and accelerate the charge transfer, which is confirmed by PL spectroscopy, TR-PL and EIS. In addition, the bimetallic ZnNi/CN photocatalyst has superior hydrogen production stability, a very promising photocatalyst. Meanwhile, this work provides a new idea for the combination of bimetallic nanoparticles with CN or other semiconductors to form excellent photocatalysts.

Li-Juan Sun: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yan-Ming Jia: Software, Formal analysis. Fu Yang: Software, Formal analysis. Zhi-Yan Bai: Software, Formal analysis. Yu-Long Xie: Software, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work. There is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled. All authors declare that no conflict of interest exists.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Research Program of Application Foundation of Qinghai Province (No. 2024-ZJ-787).
